postCopy.instagram: Between the last set and closing time, someone collected a debt the hard way. 🎷🔍 Case #044 — Last Call. Play free at thedailywhodunit.com
postCopy.tiktok: Free daily murder mystery game: a 1935 New Orleans jazz club, a body found after the music stopped, and an alibi chain with one weak link. Daily deduction puzzle — solve it at thedailywhodunit.com

marketing.imagePrompt: Interior of a smoky 1935 New Orleans jazz club after hours, brass instruments gleaming on the bandstand, overturned chairs and half-empty glasses on dark wood tables. A single lamp burns over the bar. A man's silhouette slumped in a corner booth. Cigarette smoke curling through amber light. Color palette: deep amber, charcoal, muted gold, shadow blue. Cinematic noir, style of Edward Hopper meets Weegee.
marketing.animationPrompt: Slow drift through a deserted 1935 New Orleans jazz club after closing. A trumpet lies on an empty chair. Cigarette smoke curls upward in the still air. The camera finds a corner booth — a glass still half-full, a hand visible at the edge of the table. Hold. The lamp above the bar flickers once and steadies. Fade to black.

rookie.title: Last Call
rookie.setting: Club Magnolia · French Quarter · New Orleans · 1935
rookie.flavor: Lenny Doucet was found dead in the corner booth of Club Magnolia an hour after the last set ended. He owed money to half the room and favours to the other half. Three people were still in the building when the body was found. Each one says someone else can prove they weren't near the booth.

items.name: Vince Arcand
items.detail: The club's lead horn player — Lenny owed him six months of borrowed wages.

items.name: Della Marsh
items.detail: The club's singer, last to leave the stage and first to find the body.

items.name: Remy Fontenot
items.detail: The barman who closed out the register and locked the front door.

items.name: The Corner Booth
items.detail: Where Lenny's body was found — isolated, away from the bar.

items.name: The Back Storeroom
items.detail: Where Remy was counting stock after closing.

items.name: The Bandstand
items.detail: Where Vince claims he was packing his horn after the last set.

items.name: Ice Pick
items.detail: A bar tool — single wound, consistent with the puncture found.

items.name: Whiskey Bottle
items.detail: Heavy glass bottle — blunt force trauma to the base of the skull.

items.name: Piano Wire
items.detail: A length of wire from the upright piano — ligature marks on the neck.

clues.heading: Della's Account
clues.text: Della Marsh says she was in the dressing room behind the stage from the end of the last set until she walked out to find Lenny dead — about forty minutes. She says she heard Vince packing his horn on the bandstand the whole time because he always bangs the case latches. She didn't hear him leave.
clues.alone: Della claims Vince was on the bandstand the entire time — her account depends on sound, not sight.

links.insight: Della's claim that she heard Vince on the bandstand and the discovery that Vince's case latches were already fastened and cold together reveal her account is false — Vince had finished packing long before she claims to have heard him.

clues.heading: The Ice Pick Wound
clues.text: The coroner confirms Lenny Doucet died from a single ice pick wound — precise, delivered from behind while he sat in the booth. The wound angle indicates the killer stood behind the booth and struck downward. The club's bar ice pick was found wiped and returned to the well — but trace blood remained in the handle seam.
clues.alone: Lenny was killed with the bar's own ice pick, delivered from behind — the killer knew exactly where Lenny was sitting and approached unseen.

links.insight: The bar ice pick returned to the well after wiping and Remy Fontenot's confirmed access to the bar well during closeout confirm Remy had both the means and the opportunity to use and return the weapon without leaving the bar area.

clues.heading: Remy's Alibi
clues.text: Remy Fontenot claims he was in the back storeroom counting stock from closing time until Della screamed. He says Vince came back there to grab a bottle he'd left and can confirm Remy was in the storeroom the whole time. Vince confirms this — he says he saw Remy counting bottles when he went to retrieve his bourbon.
clues.alone: Remy's alibi depends on Vince — and Vince's alibi depends on Della. The chain runs in a circle.

links.insight: Remy's alibi resting on Vince's corroboration and the storeroom stock count showing no discrepancy confirm the storeroom alibi was fabricated to give Remy cover for the time he spent at the corner booth.

clues.heading: The Debt Ledger
clues.text: A small notebook found in Lenny Doucet's coat records his debts in his own handwriting. Vince Arcand is owed forty dollars — six months of loans. Remy Fontenot is owed thirty dollars — an unpaid bar tab run over two years. Della Marsh is owed nothing. The amounts beside Remy's name are circled twice and marked final notice.
clues.alone: Lenny owed Remy thirty dollars and had marked it final notice — Remy had recently escalated his collection efforts.

links.insight: The debt ledger showing Remy's circled final notice and the threatening note found in Lenny's booth written in Remy's hand confirm Remy had made a direct ultimatum to Lenny that evening — the killing followed a refused demand.

clues.heading: The Fastened Case
clues.text: When investigators examine Vince Arcand's horn case on the bandstand, all three latches are fastened and the case is cold to the touch — consistent with being closed for at least an hour. If Vince had been packing his horn during the forty minutes Della claims to have heard him, the case would still be warm from his hands and possibly open.
clues.alone: Vince's horn case was cold and fully latched — he finished packing long before Della claims to have heard him still on the bandstand.

links.insight: Della's claim that she heard Vince on the bandstand and the discovery that Vince's case latches were already fastened and cold confirm her account is false — she fabricated Vince's presence to cover the period when she was not in the dressing room.

clues.heading: The Ice Pick Return
clues.text: The bar ice pick was found in the well, wiped with a bar rag. The rag shows a faint blood transfer consistent with a single wipe. Remy Fontenot is the only person with routine access to the bar well — it sits below the bar counter, invisible from the dining floor and inaccessible to customers.
clues.alone: The murder weapon was returned to a location only Remy routinely accesses — he was the only person who could have replaced it without being observed.

links.insight: The bar ice pick returned to the well after wiping and Remy's confirmed access to the bar well during closeout confirm Remy had both the means and the opportunity to use and return the weapon without leaving the bar area.

clues.heading: The Untouched Stock Sheet
clues.text: The storeroom stock count sheet — which Remy claims to have been completing during the murder window — is found blank except for the header. If Remy had been counting for forty minutes, the sheet would show at least partial entries. The pencil beside it shows no wear consistent with recent use.
clues.alone: Remy's storeroom alibi is physically impossible — he never started the stock count he claims was his alibi activity.

links.insight: Remy's alibi resting on Vince's corroboration and the blank stock sheet proving Remy never counted anything confirm the storeroom alibi was fabricated — Remy was not in the storeroom during the window he claims.

clues.heading: The Note in the Booth
clues.text: Tucked under Lenny's glass in the corner booth, investigators find a folded note reading: Tonight's the night you square up, Lenny. I'm done waiting. The handwriting matches the bar's nightly receipts — written in Remy Fontenot's hand. The note was placed before Lenny sat down.
clues.alone: Remy left a threatening collection note in Lenny's booth before Lenny arrived — he had planned the confrontation in advance.

links.insight: The debt ledger showing Remy's circled final notice and the threatening note in Remy's hand found in the booth confirm Remy had escalated to a direct ultimatum that evening — the killing followed a refused demand for payment.

rookie.epilogue: Remy Fontenot had waited two years for thirty dollars Lenny kept promising and never paying. He left the note in the booth before service, let the last set finish, then walked to the corner booth while Della was in her dressing room and Vince had already packed and gone for a smoke outside. He struck Lenny with the bar ice pick from behind, wiped it on the bar rag, returned it to the well, and walked back to the storeroom — never touching the stock sheet. The blank count sheet was the detail that broke the chain.

detective.title: Last Call
detective.setting: Club Magnolia · French Quarter · New Orleans · 1935
detective.flavor: The alibi chain runs Della to Vince to Remy and back to Della — a perfect triangle of mutual confirmation. Except the chain has a gap. Find where the forty minutes actually went and the whole structure collapses onto one person.
detective.emptyState: Every alibi points to someone else. One of them is lying.

items.name: Vince Arcand
items.detail: Lead horn — Lenny owed him forty dollars, alibi provided by Della.

items.name: Della Marsh
items.detail: Singer — no debt, alibi is the dressing room, confirmed by no one.

items.name: Remy Fontenot
items.detail: Barman — thirty dollar debt, storeroom alibi confirmed by Vince.

items.name: Cal Tureaud
items.detail: Club pianist — left before the last set ended, alibi unverified.

items.name: The Corner Booth
items.detail: Primary crime scene — Lenny found here, ice pick wound from behind.

items.name: The Back Storeroom
items.detail: Remy's claimed location — blank stock sheet, pencil unused.

items.name: The Bandstand
items.detail: Vince's claimed location — cold, fastened case contradicts account.

items.name: The Dressing Room
items.detail: Della's claimed location — no witness, window overlooking alley.

items.name: Ice Pick
items.detail: Bar tool — wound confirmed, returned to well after wiping.

items.name: Whiskey Bottle
items.detail: Heavy glass — no blood trace found on any bottle in the club.

items.name: Piano Wire
items.detail: No ligature marks on Lenny's neck — ruled out by coroner.

items.name: Straight Razor
items.detail: Found in Vince's case — clean, no trace evidence.

items.name: During the Last Set
items.detail: While music was playing — multiple witnesses in the room.

items.name: Between Sets
items.detail: Brief intermission — staff moving, lights up, patrons at bar.

items.name: After the Last Set
items.detail: After music ended, patrons leaving, staff winding down.

items.name: After Closing
items.detail: Door locked, only three staff remaining — the murder window.

clues.heading: The Alibi Triangle
clues.text: Della says Vince was on the bandstand. Vince says Remy was in the storeroom. Remy says Della screamed and he came running. Each alibi depends on the previous — but none of the three can be independently confirmed by anyone outside the triangle. The last patron left before closing and can only confirm all three were still inside.
clues.alone: The three alibis form a closed loop with no external verification — at least one link must be false for the chain to hold together.

links.insight: The alibi triangle with no external verification and Vince's horn case confirmed cold and latched long before Della claims to have heard him confirm Della's account is the false link — she fabricated Vince's presence on the bandstand.

clues.heading: The Wound and the Reach
clues.text: The coroner's report specifies the ice pick wound angle — fifteen degrees downward, delivered by someone standing directly behind the booth's high back. The booth's configuration means the killer approached from the kitchen corridor, not the main floor. Only the bar staff knew the kitchen corridor accessed the booth from behind.
clues.alone: The killing approach required knowledge of the kitchen corridor route — only bar staff would use or know this path to the corner booth.

links.insight: The approach route requiring bar staff knowledge of the kitchen corridor and Remy's confirmed familiarity with every back passage in the club confirm only Remy could have reached the booth from behind without crossing the main floor where Della or Vince might have seen him.

clues.heading: The Debt and the Deadline
clues.text: Lenny's debt ledger combined with the threatening note in the booth establishes a clear escalation: Remy's debt marked final notice two weeks ago, the note placed before service began that evening. A club regular interviewed the next day recalls Remy leaning over Lenny's table during the intermission between sets and the exchange becoming heated.
clues.alone: Remy confronted Lenny publicly between sets — the killing followed a heated exchange that evening, not a spontaneous decision.

links.insight: Remy's heated intermission confrontation with Lenny and the blank storeroom stock sheet proving he was not counting during the murder window confirm Remy left the bar area after closing, killed Lenny via the kitchen corridor, and fabricated the storeroom alibi.

clues.heading: The Dressing Room Window
clues.text: The dressing room window overlooks the alley beside the club. A cigarette stub consistent with Vince Arcand's preferred brand — Picayune — was found on the alley ground below the window. Vince admits he went outside for a smoke after packing his horn but says he used the side door, not the alley. The stub is in the wrong location for the side door.
clues.alone: Vince was in the alley below the dressing room window at some point — placing him outside, not on the bandstand, during part of the murder window.

links.insight: The Picayune stub below the dressing room window placing Vince in the alley and Della's admission that she saw Vince leave through the side door confirm Vince was outside during the murder window — Della's claim that she heard him on the bandstand was a deliberate lie.

clues.heading: The Cold Case
clues.text: Vince's horn case is cold and all three latches are fastened. A musician who plays a brass instrument leaves warmth in the metal fittings for thirty to forty minutes after handling. The case temperature is consistent with being closed for over an hour — not the twenty minutes since the last set ended.
clues.alone: Vince finished packing at least an hour before investigators arrived — he was gone from the bandstand long before Della claims to have heard him there.

links.insight: The alibi triangle with no external verification and Vince's cold latched case confirming he left the bandstand far earlier than Della claims together expose Della's account as the false link — she lied about hearing Vince to create an alibi window for herself or to cover Remy.

clues.heading: The Blank Sheet and the Pencil
clues.text: The storeroom stock sheet is blank below the header. The pencil shows no graphite transfer on the tip — unused. A forty-minute stock count in that storeroom would require at minimum twenty line entries. Remy could not have been counting stock. His claimed alibi activity left no physical trace.
clues.alone: Remy's stock count never happened — the blank sheet and unused pencil prove he was not in the storeroom during the window he claims.

links.insight: The approach route requiring kitchen corridor knowledge and the blank storeroom sheet proving Remy was not counting stock confirm Remy used the kitchen corridor to reach the booth from behind during the window his alibi was meant to cover.

clues.heading: The Regular's Account
clues.text: A club regular — interviewed the following morning — describes watching Remy lean across Lenny's table during the intermission, voice low, expression hard. Lenny waved him off and turned back to his drink. The regular assumed it was a bar tab dispute. He left before the last set finished and did not witness the aftermath.
clues.alone: Remy confronted Lenny face-to-face during the intermission and was dismissed — the killing followed a personal refusal, not a random opportunity.

links.insight: Remy's heated intermission confrontation dismissed by Lenny and the blank storeroom sheet proving he fabricated his alibi confirm Remy returned to settle the debt after closing, using the kitchen corridor approach to avoid witnesses.

clues.heading: Della's Revision
clues.text: Under extended questioning, Della Marsh revises her account: she admits she saw Vince leave through the side door shortly after the last set ended and heard him go. She had assumed he came back — but she did not actually hear latch sounds. She says she was covering for Vince because she assumed he had done it and didn't want him arrested.
clues.alone: Della fabricated Vince's bandstand presence to protect him — but in doing so, she also inadvertently covered the real killer's window.

links.insight: The Picayune stub placing Vince in the alley and Della's revised admission that she saw him leave confirm Vince was outside during the murder window — Della's false alibi for Vince accidentally gave Remy unscrutinised time to use the kitchen corridor.

detective.epilogue: Remy Fontenot waited until the door was locked and the room was emptying. He walked the kitchen corridor to the corner booth, struck Lenny from behind with the bar ice pick, wiped it on the well rag, and returned to the storeroom — never touching the stock sheet. Della had lied to protect Vince, not knowing Vince was in the alley and couldn't have done it. Vince's lie about using the side door pointed investigators back to the alley. The blank sheet unravelled Remy completely.

inspector.title: Last Call
inspector.setting: Club Magnolia · French Quarter · New Orleans · 1935
inspector.flavor: Remy killed Lenny. But Della's lie — built to protect Vince — was too convenient to be coincidental. Someone told Della to give Vince an alibi whether he needed one or not. And the person who told her knew exactly what was going to happen in that corner booth after closing.
inspector.emptyState: Remy pulled the pick. Someone else built the alibi around it.

items.name: Remy Fontenot
items.detail: The killer — kitchen corridor, ice pick, blank stock sheet.

items.name: Della Marsh
items.detail: Lied about Vince's alibi — claims she was protecting him.

items.name: Vince Arcand
items.detail: In the alley during the murder — his debt gives him motive too.

items.name: Cal Tureaud
items.detail: Left early — but was seen speaking to Remy before the last set.

items.name: Mae Tureaud
items.detail: Cal's wife — manages the club's books, controls who gets paid.

items.name: The Corner Booth
items.detail: Crime scene — ice pick from behind via kitchen corridor.

items.name: The Back Storeroom
items.detail: Remy's fabricated alibi location — blank sheet, unused pencil.

items.name: The Bandstand
items.detail: Vince's cold case — he left before Della claims she heard him.

items.name: The Dressing Room
items.detail: Della's location — window to alley, no witness, phone on the table.

items.name: The Kitchen Corridor
items.detail: Remy's approach route — only bar staff know this passage.

items.name: Ice Pick
items.detail: Bar tool — confirmed weapon, returned to well after wiping.

items.name: Whiskey Bottle
items.detail: No blood trace on any bottle — ruled out.

items.name: Piano Wire
items.detail: No ligature marks — coroner ruled out strangulation.

items.name: Straight Razor
items.detail: Found in Vince's case — clean, no trace evidence.

items.name: Broken Glass
items.detail: Shattered tumbler near the booth — secondary wound on Lenny's palm.

items.name: During the Last Set
items.detail: Multiple witnesses present — too public.

items.name: Between Sets

items.name: After the Last Set
items.detail: Patrons leaving — possible but Lenny still visible from the bar.

items.name: After Closing
items.detail: Door locked, three staff only — confirmed murder window.

items.name: Provided the False Alibi
items.detail: Gave Vince a cover story whether he needed one or not.

items.name: Cleared the Room
items.detail: Ensured the club emptied and only the right people remained.

items.name: Arranged the Debt Confrontation
items.detail: Set up the evening so Lenny would be alone in the booth at closing.

items.name: Withheld the Phone Record
items.detail: Suppressed a call made from the dressing room during the window.

items.name: Disposed of Evidence
items.detail: Removed or concealed materials linking a second person to the scene.

clues.heading: Della's Prepared Lie
clues.text: Della Marsh's false alibi for Vince was not improvised — when investigators first questioned her, she described the latch sounds with specific detail: three distinct clicks in sequence, the order she always heard Vince fasten them. But the case has three latches that fasten simultaneously, not in sequence. She rehearsed a story she had never actually witnessed.
clues.alone: Della described latch sounds she could not have heard — her alibi for Vince was scripted in advance, not recalled from memory.

links.insight: Della's rehearsed false alibi for Vince and the telephone exchange record showing a call from the dressing room to Remy's residence two hours before closing together confirm Della and Remy coordinated the false alibi before the murder took place.

clues.heading: The Dressing Room Call
clues.text: The club's telephone exchange log — obtained from the French Quarter operator — shows a call placed from the dressing room extension to a residential number two hours before closing. The residential number is registered to Remy Fontenot's rooming house. The call lasted eleven minutes. Della claims she made no calls that evening.
clues.alone: Della called Remy's rooming house two hours before closing — an eleven-minute call she denies making.

links.insight: The eleven-minute call from Della to Remy before closing and Cal Tureaud's confirmed conversation with Remy before the last set together establish that at least two people were coordinating with Remy in the hours before the murder.

clues.heading: Cal's Conversation
clues.text: A kitchen worker who stayed late to clean overhears Cal Tureaud speaking to Remy before the last set — specifically the phrase he'll be in the booth by midnight, same as always. Cal denies the conversation. When pressed, he admits Lenny always sat in the corner booth after closing to finish his drink alone — a habit the regular staff all knew.
clues.alone: Cal told Remy that Lenny would be in the corner booth at midnight — either sharing known information or actively coordinating the ambush.

links.insight: Cal's confirmation that Lenny would be in the booth at midnight and Mae Tureaud's control of the club's payment ledger confirm the Tureauds had a financial interest in Lenny's death that predates the evening.

clues.heading: Mae's Ledger Manipulation
clues.text: The club's payment ledger — maintained by Mae Tureaud — shows Lenny Doucet's bar tab debt to Remy was never formally recorded as a club liability. Mae had kept it off the books, ensuring it could never be settled through the club's accounts. This meant Lenny could only repay Remy personally — keeping the debt a private matter between them.
clues.alone: Mae Tureaud deliberately kept Lenny's debt off the official books — ensuring it remained a personal grievance between Lenny and Remy that could escalate without institutional resolution.

links.insight: Mae's manipulation of the ledger to keep Lenny's debt personal and the insurance policy naming Mae as beneficiary with a dispute-extinguishment clause confirm the Tureauds stood to gain from Lenny's death and engineered the conditions for Remy's confrontation.

clues.heading: The Script in the Mirror
clues.text: A small piece of paper tucked into the dressing room mirror frame — missed in the initial search — contains three lines in Della's handwriting: Was in dressing room. Heard Vince — three clicks, left to right. Didn't hear Remy move. It is a crib sheet for her alibi. The paper is dry and the ink is not fresh — it was written before the murder.
clues.alone: Della wrote her alibi script before the murder occurred — she knew in advance what she would need to say.

links.insight: Della's scripted latch detail that doesn't match the case's actual mechanism and the pre-written alibi crib sheet confirm she was briefed on what to say before the murder — she was a prepared accomplice, not a spontaneous liar.

clues.heading: What the Regular Overheard
clues.text: The club regular who witnessed Remy's intermission confrontation with Lenny also recalls — upon extended questioning — hearing Della say to Remy as she passed the bar after the last set: It's done. He's staying. She then went directly to the dressing room. The regular did not understand the significance at the time.
clues.alone: Della confirmed to Remy after the last set that Lenny was staying in the booth — she was coordinating the timing of his approach.

links.insight: The eleven-minute call from Della to Remy before closing and her post-set confirmation that Lenny was staying confirm Della was Remy's active coordinator — she monitored Lenny's position and signalled when the window was open.

clues.heading: The Tureaud Motive
clues.text: A separate financial record found in Mae Tureaud's desk drawer shows Lenny Doucet had a silent ownership stake in Club Magnolia — ten percent, granted to him by the original owner before the Tureauds purchased the club. Lenny had recently engaged an attorney to formalise the stake. His death extinguishes the claim before it could be registered.
clues.alone: Lenny had a ten percent ownership stake in the club that would have cost the Tureauds significantly — his death before registration extinguishes the claim entirely.

links.insight: Cal's confirmation of Lenny's booth position at midnight and the Tureauds' financial exposure from Lenny's ownership claim confirm the Tureauds arranged the conditions of the murder — they needed Lenny dead before his attorney could file the stake registration.

clues.heading: The Insurance Connection
clues.text: A business insurance policy on Club Magnolia's goodwill assets — filed six weeks prior — names Mae Tureaud as sole beneficiary and includes a clause that any unregistered ownership disputes are extinguished upon a principal's death. The policy was taken out two weeks after Lenny's attorney sent a letter to the club requesting documentation of the original ownership agreement.
clues.alone: Mae took out an insurance policy with a dispute-extinguishment clause two weeks after receiving Lenny's attorney's letter — she had anticipated and prepared for Lenny's death.

links.insight: Mae's deliberate off-book ledger manipulation keeping Lenny's debt unresolvable and her insurance policy with a dispute-extinguishment clause confirm she orchestrated Remy as the instrument of a killing that served her financial interests — Remy's debt grievance was the weapon she aimed.

inspector.epilogue: Mae Tureaud had engineered it from the start. She kept Lenny's debt off the books to keep Remy's grievance raw, took out the insurance policy after his attorney's letter arrived, and used Cal to confirm Lenny's booth position to Remy before the last set. Della — who owed Mae a favour from a prior incident — was briefed on what to say and wrote her crib sheet that afternoon. Remy killed Lenny for thirty dollars. Mae extinguished a ten percent ownership claim worth several thousand. Remy confessed within forty-eight hours. Della's crib sheet was the key. Mae was indicted for criminal conspiracy three months later. Cal was never charged.
